---
title: "df and du different - lost 145GB of disk space"
date: 2008-04-26
forum: General Help
---

### Post by ironwolfe on 2008-04-26
I should start with the basics of the problem:

- main hard disk (sdb1) is showing suddenly that it is nearly full see df below:

```
ian@ian-desktop:~$ df -h
Filesystem            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sdb1             453G  335G   95G  78% /
varrun               1014M  260K 1014M   1% /var/run
varlock              1014M  4.0K 1014M   1% /var/lock
udev                 1014M  112K 1014M   1% /dev
devshm               1014M     0 1014M   0% /dev/shm
lrm                  1014M   34M  980M   4% /lib/modules/2.6.22-14-generic/volatile
/dev/sdd5             466G  447G   19G  96% /media/Backup500
/dev/sdc1             276G   82G  181G  32% /media/Backup300
/dev/sda1             367G  177G  172G  51% /media/Backup400
/dev/sde1             459G   80G  356G  19% /media/HD500
/dev/hda              2.1G  2.1G     0 100% /media/cdrom0

```

Yes, I have a few hard drives.  - As you can see it says I have 335 GB used.

Now du -h --max-depth=1 /  says I am only using about 190 GB

```
ian@ian-desktop:~$ sudo du -h --max-depth=1 /
[sudo] password for ian:
4.0K    /initrd
2.2M    /root
6.6M    /sbin
158M    /lib
12M     /etc
0       /sys
4.7M    /bin
4.0K    /mnt
4.0K    /workfiles
4.0K    /srv
124K    /dev
145G    /home
31G     /var
12K     /Recycled
787G    /media
152M    /opt
6.4M    /tmp
16K     /lost+found
0       /proc
3.4G    /usr
18M     /boot
965G    /

```
Things I have done recently:
1.  changed my fstab to add a UUID for a USB drive - no success so I changed it back.
2.  tried hibernation (earth day), and stopped the hibernation process in mid stream
3.  crashed audacity while it was handling a large file. 


Things I know (google) or have tried:
1.  du and df look at different things (disk usage vs. file system)
2.  I have lsof | grep 'deleted' and lsof | grep 'tmp' I have "killed" all the processes I could find hoping that some file is stranded.  no luck
3.  fsck from live CD
4.  chkrootkit - nothing found
5.  commented out all the other drives in the fstab - no luck
6.  gparted from Live CD - shows same as df
7.  

here is my fstab:

```
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sdb1 :
UUID=c674f9fb-e4d7-4abf-9d2d-ccc6533cd86c / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
#/dev/sdb1 / etx3 ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sdb5 :
#UUID=f086d1e2-5449-45b4-a7fe-4179f98e8e26 none swap sw 0 0
#/dev/sdb5 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/hda /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto,exec 0 0
/dev/sdd5 /media/Backup500 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sdc1 /media/Backup300 ext3 defaults 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/Backup400 ext2 defaults 0 0
/dev/sde1 /media/HD500 ext3 defaults 0 0
``` 

Here is my mtab:

```
/dev/sdb1 / ext3 rw,errors=remount-ro 0 0
proc /proc proc rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev 0 0
/sys /sys sysfs rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev 0 0
varrun /var/run tmpfs rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev,mode=0755 0 0
varlock /var/lock tmpfs rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev,mode=1777 0 0
udev /dev tmpfs rw,mode=0755 0 0
devshm /dev/shm tmpfs rw 0 0
devpts /dev/pts devpts rw,gid=5,mode=620 0 0
lrm /lib/modules/2.6.22-14-generic/volatile tmpfs rw 0 0
/dev/sdd5 /media/Backup500 fuseblk rw,nosuid,nodev,allow_other,blksize=4096 0 0
/dev/sdc1 /media/Backup300 ext3 rw 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/Backup400 ext2 rw 0 0
/dev/sde1 /media/HD500 ext3 rw 0 0
securityfs /sys/kernel/security securityfs rw 0 0

binfmt_misc /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc binfmt_misc rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev 0 0
/dev/hda /media/cdrom0 udf ro,nosuid,nodev,user=ian 0 0
```

### Post by kidders on 2008-04-27
Hi there,

> **ironwolfe said:**
> Now du -h --max-depth=1 /  says I am only using about 190 GBI'm curious ... How did you come up with the 190GB figure?

It might take a while to run this, but what's the output of... ```
sudo du -hsx /
```(The "sudo" is only necessary if you want to include files that unprivileged users can't access.)

---

### Post by ironwolfe on 2008-04-28
> **kidders said:**
> 
I'm curious ... How did you come up with the 190GB figure?

It might take a while to run this, but what's the output of... ```
sudo du -hsx /
```(The "sudo" is only necessary if you want to include files that unprivileged users can't access.)

Thanks for replying - I was beginning to think the community had abandoned me.

The total is 335GB - (140GB+5GB) = 190GB.  I realize now that I missed /var which has 30GB (VMware machines). so the total would be 160GB - still alot...

```
ian@ian-desktop:~$ sudo du -hsx /
183G    /

```

**New Information**
My machine no longer hibernates - it goes into it and then comes back out.  I have a feeling this is the cause.  I checked lsof | grep hald but nothing out of the ordinary.  Is there a file it makes on the drive that might still be there?

Thanks,
Ian

---

### Post by kidders on 2008-04-28
Drat... I was hoping there'd be some sort of mistake, but your estimate of the amount of space really in use is pretty close. As one last-ditch effort to find a reasonable explanation, could you post the output of...```
sudo dumpe2fs -h /dev/sdb1
```I doubt that'll reveal anything tbh ... if you'd done anything funny while creating the filesystem, you'd probably have mentioned it ... but it's at least worth a quick look.

So, I'd be tempted to recreate your root filesystem, if I were you. You could...
[LIST]
[*]Boot from something other than your Ubuntu installation ... eg a LiveCD.
[*]Run fsck on /dev/sdb1, just in case doing so magically recovers the missing disk space.
[*]If not, copy your entire root filesystem to /dev/sde1. Something like **sudo cp -dpRx /* /media/HD500** ought to do the trick ... or you could just tarball the filesystem.
[*]Re-run mkfs on /dev/sdb1.
[*]Copy everything back again.
[*]Update anywhere (eg /etc/fstab) that contains a reference to your old root filesystem's UUID, or use tune2fs to change the new UUID back to the old one.
[*]Unmount all your filesystems & reboot.
[/LIST]
This is all a bit drastic though (and prone to screw-ups, if it's not something you've done before), which is why I'm double-checking that it's really necessary.

---

I ran the fsck again again it was clean.  My gut is telling me it is a hibernation file that is stuck - nothing in the lsof though....

I have started the backup of the /home directories and /var and think this may be a very good reason to move to Hardy Heron... I just didn't want to go through all the installation and tweaking again.  ssh, fancontrol, apps, oh well.  I could do the mkfs but if I am going to do that I might as well upgrade ;)

If you get any ideas, I won't be upgrading for a few days, please post them.

Thanks again.

### Post by ghost_ryder35 on 2008-04-29
if i remember right the hibernation file is written to /swap not the / or /home

---

### Post by kidders on 2008-04-29
> **ghost_ryder35 said:**
> if i remember right the hibernation file is written to /swap not the / or /homeThat's true.

The superblock dump ironwolfe posted does explain where *some* of the missing space went (approximately 23G of reserved blocks), but there's still far too much missing. One thing I don't understand though is the "First orphan inode" entry. I wasn't aware it was possible to fsck a filesystem and still have orphaned inodes in it. Naturally, I may be wrong, but that suggests a broken filesystem to me.

:-(

### Post by ironwolfe on 2008-05-01
I love the highly technical "broken filesystem"...

I am running a copy right now of entire filesystem.  I will then mkfs and start again.

I am running the boot manager on the same linux drive (the same one that is corrupted, is there anything I should do to make sure Grub loads properly after I copy all the files back?

I also changed the fstab to all UUID - they all mount but it doesn't fix the problem.

---

### Post by kidders on 2008-05-01
> **ironwolfe said:**
> is there anything I should do to make sure Grub loads properly after I copy all the files back?That depends on whether you installed your Grub into your disk's MBR. If in doubt, double-check its configuration for anything that depends on your old root filesystem (eg a reference to its UUID), and reinstall it.

---

### Post by ironwolfe on 2008-05-08
For those of you that are still following this thread - I finally just copied all my files over to my spare drive - reformatted the drive and it all works now.

I should note if you do this you have to go back into your /etc/fstab file and update the UUID (if you are using it).

Determine the /dev file that you just reformatted by typing this into a terminal:

```
sudo fdisk -l
```

The command is
```
sudo vol_id -u /dev/sda1
```

copy the UUID into the fstab.

Otherwise your new filesystem will not load

---
